steps.title: ADS Component Overview
steps.objective: Identify the core components that make up an ADS system.
keyIdeas: The list of ADS components: CPC, main protective bonding, supplementary bonding, earthing conductor, protective devices, and earth electrode.
ADS is the primary method of protection against electric shock; knowing the parts is the first step to ensuring they are present.

Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) is not a single device, but a coordinated safety strategy designed to prevent electric shock. It works by ensuring that if a fault occurs, the power is cut off almost instantly. For ADS to function, several components must work together as a system.

The core parts of this system include the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC), which runs to individual points like sockets, and the Earthing Conductor, which connects the whole building to the earth source. It also includes protective devices like fuses or circuit breakers that physically trip the power. Finally, it involves bonding conductors (main and supplementary) and, in some systems, an earth electrode (a rod in the ground). If any one of these parts is missing or broken, the 'safety shield' of ADS may fail to protect the user during a fault.

steps.title: Bonding and Protective Devices
steps.objective: Distinguish between protective devices and bonding conductors within the ADS framework.
keyIdeas: The specific roles of protective devices (fuses/MCBs) versus bonding conductors (main and supplementary).
Confusing bonding with circuit protection leads to dangerous installation errors.

In an ADS system, different components have very specific roles. Protective devices, such as fuses and Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCBs), are the 'switches' that automatically turn off the electricity when they detect a fault current that is too high. Their job is to break the circuit to stop the danger.

Bonding conductors have a different purpose. Main protective bonding conductors connect incoming metal services, such as gas or water pipes, to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET). Supplementary bonding is used in high-risk areas like bathrooms to connect touchable metal parts together. Unlike a CPC, bonding conductors are not designed to carry fault current back from a broken appliance. Instead, they keep all metalwork at the same voltage (equipotential). This prevents a person from getting a shock if they touch two different metal surfaces at the same time during a fault.
Explanation B

steps.title: TT Earthing Systems
steps.objective: Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.
keyIdeas: TT systems rely on the mass of earth for the fault path, requiring a consumer-owned earth electrode.
TT systems are common in rural areas; failing to identify one means failing to install the necessary electrode.

Earthing systems are identified by letter codes. In a TT system, the first 'T' stands for 'Terra' (the Latin word for Earth), indicating the source transformer is connected to earth. The second 'T' means the consumer's installation is also connected to earth via its own local connection.

In a TT arrangement, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal to the property. Because of this, the consumer must provide their own 'means of earthing' by installing an earth electrode. This is typically a copper-clad steel rod hammered deep into the soil. Because the fault current has to travel through the actual mass of the earth (soil and rocks) to get back to the transformer, the resistance is much higher than in metallic systems. Consequently, TT systems almost always require a Residual Current Device (RCD) to ensure the power shuts off fast enough to be safe.

steps.title: TN-S and TN-C-S Systems
steps.objective: Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using the code letters and supply path.
keyIdeas: TN-S (Separate neutral and earth throughout) vs TN-C-S (Combined neutral and earth in the supply, separated at the intake).
The supply type dictates how the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) is connected to the supplier's equipment.

TN systems use the supplier's cable to provide a low-resistance metallic path for fault current. In a TN-S system, the 'S' stands for 'Separate'. This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are two completely distinct wires running all the way from the transformer to the consumer's intake. The earth is often provided by the metal sheath of the supplier's cable.

In a TN-C-S system, the 'C' stands for 'Combined' and the 'S' for 'Separate'. In this setup, the supplier uses one single wire (a PEN conductor) to act as both Neutral and Earth in the street. However, when this cable reaches the consumer's property, the combined wire is split into a separate Neutral and Earth at the cutout. This system is very common in the UK and is often referred to as PME (Protective Multiple Earthing) because the supplier earths the combined conductor at multiple points along the network.

steps.title: CPC and Earthing Conductor
steps.objective: Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.
keyIdeas: The Earthing Conductor connects the MET to the means of earthing; the CPC connects the MET to the exposed parts of a circuit.
Using the wrong term during testing or installation leads to non-compliance and confusion in documentation.

It is vital to use the correct names for conductors to avoid confusion during testing and installation. The Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) is the specific wire that runs within an individual circuit. For example, the small green and yellow wire inside a 'twin and earth' cable that connects a metal light fitting to the consumer unit is a CPC.

The Earthing Conductor is different; it is the main, thick green and yellow wire that connects the entire installation's Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the actual means of earthing (like the supplier's earth terminal or an earth rod). To remember the difference, think of the CPC as a 'branch' that protects one specific circuit, while the Earthing Conductor is the 'trunk' that connects the whole system's earth terminal to the ground source.

steps.title: Worked Example
steps.objective: Identify the earthing system type and the specific ADS components required for a rural installation.
keyIdeas: Show one worked example.

Scenario: You are inspecting a small detached workshop in a rural area. You notice that the electricity supplier's incoming cable (the cutout) does not have an earth terminal provided. Outside, you find a copper-clad steel rod driven into the ground with a 16mm² green and yellow cable connected to it, which runs back to a terminal block inside the consumer unit.

Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Because the supplier has not provided an earth terminal and the installation relies on a local connection to the ground (the rod), this is a TT system (Terra-Terra).
Step 2: Identify the 'Means of Earthing'. The copper-clad rod in the ground is the Earth Electrode.
Step 3: Identify the main connection cable. The 16mm² green and yellow cable connecting that rod to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) is the Earthing Conductor.
Step 4: Identify the circuit-level protection. The 1.5mm² green and yellow wire inside the 'twin and earth' cable feeding the workshop lights is the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC).

steps: Identify the earthing system type from the scenario.

steps.questionText: Which earthing system is described in this scenario?
steps.hint: A local earth electrode points to TT.

steps.title: Practice: Identifying TN-C-S
steps.objective: Identify TN-C-S from the combined-and-separated supply arrangement.
keyIdeas: TN-C-S combines neutral and earth on part of the supply.
The conductor is separated into neutral and earth at the service position.
anchorFacts: A PEN conductor means protective earth and neutral are combined.
TN-C-S is also known as PME.
taskConstraints: Keep the scenario focused on combined then separated supply conductors.
Require one earthing-system answer only.
steps.questionIntent: Identify TN-C-S from a combined then separated supply arrangement.

taskSkeleton.scenario: The supply neutral and earth are combined in one conductor outside the installation and separated at the service position.
steps: Identify the earthing system from the supply arrangement.

steps.questionText: Which earthing system is described in this scenario?
steps.hint: Combined first, then separated, points to TN-C-S.

steps.title: Integrative Close: Why TT Uses an RCD
steps.objective: Explain why TT systems usually need an RCD.
keyIdeas: TT systems rely on soil for the return path, which has much higher resistance than a metal return path.
High earth path resistance limits fault current, so a fuse or MCB may not trip quickly enough.
An RCD is used because it trips on imbalance rather than needing a large fault current.
anchorFacts: High soil resistance means low fault current in a TT system.
An RCD can disconnect even when the fault current is too small for a fuse or MCB to operate quickly.
taskConstraints: Keep the response to one short explanation.
Require the learner to link high resistance to low fault current and then to RCD operation.
steps.questionIntent: Explain why TT systems usually require an RCD.

taskSkeleton.scenario: A TT installation depends on a local earth electrode and the earth path is relatively high resistance.
steps: Explain why this makes an RCD necessary.

steps.questionText: Using one short explanation, explain why TT systems usually require an RCD.
steps.hint: The key issue is not the label TT by itself; it is the resistance of the earth return path.

terms.simpleDefinition: A safety strategy that uses protective devices to quickly cut off electricity during a fault to prevent electric shock.
terms.anchor: The coordinated 'safety shield' of wires and breakers that kills the power before it becomes lethal.
terms.misconception: Thinking ADS is just a single fuse; it is actually a system of conductors and devices working together.

terms.simpleDefinition: An earthing arrangement where the consumer provides their own connection to earth using a local electrode (rod).
terms.anchor: Terra-Terra: Earth at the transformer and a separate Earth rod at the house.
terms.misconception: Assuming the electricity supplier always provides an earth terminal for every property.

terms.simpleDefinition: An earthing arrangement where the neutral and earth conductors are kept separate throughout the entire network.
terms.anchor: T-N-Separate: Two distinct wires (Neutral and Earth) running all the way back to the source.
terms.misconception: Believing the neutral and earth are joined together at the consumer's main intake.

terms.simpleDefinition: An arrangement where neutral and earth are combined in the supply cable but separated at the consumer's intake.
terms.anchor: T-N-Combined-Separate: A shared wire in the street that splits into two at the property's cutout.
terms.misconception: Thinking TN-C-S and TN-S are the same because they both use the supplier's cable for the earth path.

terms.simpleDefinition: The specific wire that connects the metal parts of an electrical circuit to the Main Earthing Terminal.
terms.anchor: The green and yellow wire found inside a standard 'twin and earth' cable.
terms.misconception: Using the term 'Earthing Conductor' to describe the small earth wire inside a final circuit.

terms.simpleDefinition: The main conductor that connects the installation's Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the actual means of earthing.
terms.anchor: The 'Main Link' between the consumer unit and the supplier's earth terminal or the ground rod.
terms.misconception: Confusing this main connection with the individual CPCs that run to lights or sockets.

terms.simpleDefinition: A conductor used to connect metal services like gas or water pipes to the Main Earthing Terminal.
terms.anchor: The thick green and yellow wire clamped to the incoming metal water or gas main.
terms.misconception: Thinking bonding conductors are intended to carry fault current from a broken appliance like a CPC does.

terms.simpleDefinition: A metal rod or plate driven into the ground to provide a physical connection to the mass of Earth.
terms.anchor: The copper-clad rod hammered into the dirt, essential for TT earthing systems.
terms.misconception: Believing that every building requires a rod in the ground regardless of the supply type.

teachChecks.title: ADS Component Overview
teachChecks.objective: Identify the core components that make up an ADS system.
retrievalTextLines: Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) is not a single device, but a coordinated safety strategy designed to prevent electric shock. It works by ensuring that if a fault occurs, the power is cut off almost instantly. For ADS to function, several components must work together as a system.

The core parts of this system include the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC), which runs to individual points like sockets, and the Earthing Conductor, which connects the whole building to the earth source. It also includes protective devices like fuses or circuit breakers that physically trip the power. Finally, it involves bonding conductors (main and supplementary) and, in some systems, an earth electrode (a rod in the ground). If any one of these parts is missing or broken, the 'safety shield' of ADS may fail to protect the user during a fault.
teachChecks.hint: Think of ADS as a team of parts, including wires like the CPC and devices like fuses, rather than just one single component.

teachChecks.title: Bonding and Protective Devices
teachChecks.objective: Distinguish between protective devices and bonding conductors within the ADS framework.
retrievalTextLines: In an ADS system, different components have very specific roles. Protective devices, such as fuses and Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCBs), are the 'switches' that automatically turn off the electricity when they detect a fault current that is too high. Their job is to break the circuit to stop the danger.

Bonding conductors have a different purpose. Main protective bonding conductors connect incoming metal services, such as gas or water pipes, to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET). Supplementary bonding is used in high-risk areas like bathrooms to connect touchable metal parts together. Unlike a CPC, bonding conductors are not designed to carry fault current back from a broken appliance. Instead, they keep all metalwork at the same voltage (equipotential). This prevents a person from getting a shock if they touch two different metal surfaces at the same time during a fault.
teachChecks.hint: Protective devices like MCBs cut the power, while bonding conductors keep metal pipes at the same voltage to prevent shocks between them.

teachChecks.title: TT Earthing Systems
teachChecks.objective: Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.
retrievalTextLines: Earthing systems are identified by letter codes. In a TT system, the first 'T' stands for 'Terra' (the Latin word for Earth), indicating the source transformer is connected to earth. The second 'T' means the consumer's installation is also connected to earth via its own local connection.

In a TT arrangement, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal to the property. Because of this, the consumer must provide their own 'means of earthing' by installing an earth electrode. This is typically a copper-clad steel rod hammered deep into the soil. Because the fault current has to travel through the actual mass of the earth (soil and rocks) to get back to the transformer, the resistance is much higher than in metallic systems. Consequently, TT systems almost always require a Residual Current Device (RCD) to ensure the power shuts off fast enough to be safe.
teachChecks.hint: In a TT system, 'Terra-Terra' means both the supplier and the consumer have their own separate connections to the ground, usually via a rod.

teachChecks.title: TN-S and TN-C-S Systems
teachChecks.objective: Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using the code letters and supply path.
retrievalTextLines: TN systems use the supplier's cable to provide a low-resistance metallic path for fault current. In a TN-S system, the 'S' stands for 'Separate'. This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are two completely distinct wires running all the way from the transformer to the consumer's intake. The earth is often provided by the metal sheath of the supplier's cable.

In a TN-C-S system, the 'C' stands for 'Combined' and the 'S' for 'Separate'. In this setup, the supplier uses one single wire (a PEN conductor) to act as both Neutral and Earth in the street. However, when this cable reaches the consumer's property, the combined wire is split into a separate Neutral and Earth at the cutout. This system is very common in the UK and is often referred to as PME (Protective Multiple Earthing) because the supplier earths the combined conductor at multiple points along the network.
teachChecks.hint: TN-S has separate wires the whole way, while TN-C-S combines them in the street and separates them only when they enter the building.

teachChecks.title: CPC and Earthing Conductor
teachChecks.objective: Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.
retrievalTextLines: It is vital to use the correct names for conductors to avoid confusion during testing and installation. The Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) is the specific wire that runs within an individual circuit. For example, the small green and yellow wire inside a 'twin and earth' cable that connects a metal light fitting to the consumer unit is a CPC.

The Earthing Conductor is different; it is the main, thick green and yellow wire that connects the entire installation's Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the actual means of earthing (like the supplier's earth terminal or an earth rod). To remember the difference, think of the CPC as a 'branch' that protects one specific circuit, while the Earthing Conductor is the 'trunk' that connects the whole system's earth terminal to the ground source.
teachChecks.hint: The CPC is the wire inside a circuit cable, while the Earthing Conductor is the main link between the consumer unit and the earth source.

Critical rules:
- basicQuestions must contain exactly 3 items.
- Each basic question must test one taught fact only.
- Each basic question answerGuidance must contain 2-5 checkable phrases or ideas.
- Never ask the learner to supply multiple separate facts in one basic question.
- Prefer single-fact or single-term checks that support precise corrective feedback.
- If a question asks for the specific term, name, abbreviation expansion, code letter, or technical label, the first answerGuidance item must be the exact target from the lesson vocabulary.
- If a question defines something and asks for its name or type, answerGuidance must contain the exact technical term that matches that definition.
- For exact-term questions, answerGuidance may include only tight equivalents of that same target. Do not accept loose descriptive paraphrases, topic labels, or nearby concepts from other questions.
- If a question asks what one code letter stands for, answerGuidance must contain only that code-letter expansion word or a tight grammatical variant, not a component name or neighbouring concept.
- Keep each answerGuidance list local to its own question only. Do not copy valid answers from adjacent questions in the same step.
- The deeper question must connect the explanation facts and ask why/how/what-must-be-true.
- deeperAnswerGuidance must contain 3-6 checkable ideas.

teachChecks.title: ADS Component Overview

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the abbreviation ADS stand for?
Instead of being a single device, what term describes ADS as a coordinated safety strategy?
Which specific conductor runs to individual points like sockets to provide a path for fault current?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why would the 'safety shield' of ADS fail to protect a user if a circuit had a fuse but was missing its CPC?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The fuse requires a path for fault current to flow
Without a CPC there is no low-resistance path back to the source
The fuse would not detect the fault and would not trip
Metal parts would remain live and dangerous
teachChecks.hint: Think of ADS as a team of parts, including wires like the CPC and devices like fuses, rather than just one single component.

teachChecks.title: Bonding and Protective Devices

basicQuestions.questionText: Which components act as the 'switches' that automatically break the circuit when they detect a high fault current?
What is the name of the conductor used to connect incoming metal gas or water pipes to the Main Earthing Terminal?
What technical state is achieved when bonding conductors keep all touchable metalwork at the same voltage?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: How does bonding prevent an electric shock even though it is not designed to carry fault current back to the source like a CPC?
deeperAnswerGuidance: It eliminates the voltage difference between two metal parts
It ensures all metalwork stays at the same potential
Current cannot flow through a person if there is no potential difference
It prevents a shock from touching two different surfaces simultaneously
teachChecks.hint: Protective devices like MCBs cut the power, while bonding conductors keep metal pipes at the same voltage to prevent shocks between them.

teachChecks.title: TT Earthing Systems

basicQuestions.questionText: In the earthing code 'TT', what does the letter 'T' stand for?
What specific component must a consumer install in a TT system because the supplier does not provide an earth terminal?
Which device is almost always required in a TT system to ensure the power shuts off fast enough despite high soil resistance?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why does a TT system rely on the 'mass of earth' for its fault path more than a TN-S system does?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The supplier does not provide a metallic return path
Fault current must travel through the actual ground or soil
The connection to the source is made through the earth itself
It uses a local electrode instead of a continuous cable sheath
teachChecks.hint: In a TT system, 'Terra-Terra' means both the supplier and the consumer have their own separate connections to the ground, usually via a rod.

teachChecks.title: TN-S and TN-C-S Systems

basicQuestions.questionText: In a TN-S system, what does the letter 'S' stand for?
In a TN-C-S system, what does the letter 'C' stand for?
What is the common UK name for a TN-C-S system where the supplier earths the combined conductor at multiple points?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: At what specific location in a TN-C-S installation does the combined supply conductor split into a separate Neutral and Earth?
deeperAnswerGuidance: At the consumer's intake
At the cutout
Before the Main Earthing Terminal
Where the supply cable enters the property
teachChecks.hint: TN-S has separate wires the whole way, while TN-C-S combines them in the street and separates them only when they enter the building.

teachChecks.title: CPC and Earthing Conductor

basicQuestions.questionText: Which conductor is found inside a 'twin and earth' cable to protect a specific individual circuit?
What is the name of the main conductor that connects the entire installation's MET to the means of earthing?
What does the abbreviation MET stand for?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Using the 'tree' analogy, why is the Earthing Conductor described as the 'trunk' of the earthing system?
deeperAnswerGuidance: It is the single main link for the whole installation
All individual circuit CPCs eventually connect to it
It carries the total fault current from the MET to the earth source
It connects the central terminal to the ground connection
teachChecks.hint: The CPC is the wire inside a circuit cable, while the Earthing Conductor is the main link between the consumer unit and the earth source.

{
  "teachChecks": [
    {
      "title": "ADS Component Overview",
      "objective": "Identify the core components that make up an ADS system.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) is not a single device, but a coordinated safety strategy designed to prevent electric shock. It works by ensuring that if a fault occurs, the power is cut off almost instantly. For ADS to function, several components must work together as a system.",
        "The core parts of this system include the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC), which runs to individual points like sockets, and the Earthing Conductor, which connects the whole building to the earth source. It also includes protective devices like fuses or circuit breakers that physically trip the power. Finally, it involves bonding conductors (main and supplementary) and, in some systems, an earth electrode (a rod in the ground). If any one of these parts is missing or broken, the 'safety shield' of ADS may fail to protect the user during a fault."
      ],
      "hint": "Think of ADS as a team of parts, including wires like the CPC and devices like fuses, rather than just one single component."
    },
    {
      "title": "Bonding and Protective Devices",
      "objective": "Distinguish between protective devices and bonding conductors within the ADS framework.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "In an ADS system, different components have very specific roles. Protective devices, such as fuses and Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCBs), are the 'switches' that automatically turn off the electricity when they detect a fault current that is too high. Their job is to break the circuit to stop the danger.",
        "Bonding conductors have a different purpose. Main protective bonding conductors connect incoming metal services, such as gas or water pipes, to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET). Supplementary bonding is used in high-risk areas like bathrooms to connect touchable metal parts together. Unlike a CPC, bonding conductors are not designed to carry fault current back from a broken appliance. Instead, they keep all metalwork at the same voltage (equipotential). This prevents a person from getting a shock if they touch two different metal surfaces at the same time during a fault."
      ],
      "hint": "Protective devices like MCBs cut the power, while bonding conductors keep metal pipes at the same voltage to prevent shocks between them."
    },
    {
      "title": "TT Earthing Systems",
      "objective": "Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Earthing systems are identified by letter codes. In a TT system, the first 'T' stands for 'Terra' (the Latin word for Earth), indicating the source transformer is connected to earth. The second 'T' means the consumer's installation is also connected to earth via its own local connection.",
        "In a TT arrangement, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal to the property. Because of this, the consumer must provide their own 'means of earthing' by installing an earth electrode. This is typically a copper-clad steel rod hammered deep into the soil. Because the fault current has to travel through the actual mass of the earth (soil and rocks) to get back to the transformer, the resistance is much higher than in metallic systems. Consequently, TT systems almost always require a Residual Current Device (RCD) to ensure the power shuts off fast enough to be safe."
      ],
      "hint": "In a TT system, 'Terra-Terra' means both the supplier and the consumer have their own separate connections to the ground, usually via a rod."
    },
    {
      "title": "TN-S and TN-C-S Systems",
      "objective": "Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using the code letters and supply path.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "TN systems use the supplier's cable to provide a low-resistance metallic path for fault current. In a TN-S system, the 'S' stands for 'Separate'. This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are two completely distinct wires running all the way from the transformer to the consumer's intake. The earth is often provided by the metal sheath of the supplier's cable.",
        "In a TN-C-S system, the 'C' stands for 'Combined' and the 'S' for 'Separate'. In this setup, the supplier uses one single wire (a PEN conductor) to act as both Neutral and Earth in the street. However, when this cable reaches the consumer's property, the combined wire is split into a separate Neutral and Earth at the cutout. This system is very common in the UK and is often referred to as PME (Protective Multiple Earthing) because the supplier earths the combined conductor at multiple points along the network."
      ],
      "hint": "TN-S has separate wires the whole way, while TN-C-S combines them in the street and separates them only when they enter the building."
    },
    {
      "title": "CPC and Earthing Conductor",
      "objective": "Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "It is vital to use the correct names for conductors to avoid confusion during testing and installation. The Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) is the specific wire that runs within an individual circuit. For example, the small green and yellow wire inside a 'twin and earth' cable that connects a metal light fitting to the consumer unit is a CPC.",
        "The Earthing Conductor is different; it is the main, thick green and yellow wire that connects the entire installation's Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the actual means of earthing (like the supplier's earth terminal or an earth rod). To remember the difference, think of the CPC as a 'branch' that protects one specific circuit, while the Earthing Conductor is the 'trunk' that connects the whole system's earth terminal to the ground source."
      ],
      "hint": "The CPC is the wire inside a circuit cable, while the Earthing Conductor is the main link between the consumer unit and the earth source."
    }
  ]
}

{
  "title": "Worked Example",
  "objective": "Identify the earthing system type and the specific ADS components required for a rural installation.",
  "retrievalTextLines": [
    "Scenario: You are inspecting a small detached workshop in a rural area. You notice that the electricity supplier's incoming cable (the cutout) does not have an earth terminal provided. Outside, you find a copper-clad steel rod driven into the ground with a 16mm² green and yellow cable connected to it, which runs back to a terminal block inside the consumer unit.",

parsed.title: Worked Example
parsed.objective: Identify the earthing system type and the specific ADS components required for a rural installation.
retrievalTextLines: Scenario: You are inspecting a small detached workshop in a rural area. You notice that the electricity supplier's incoming cable (the cutout) does not have an earth terminal provided. Outside, you find a copper-clad steel rod driven into the ground with a 16mm² green and yellow cable connected to it, which runs back to a terminal block inside the consumer unit.

Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Because the supplier has not provided an earth terminal and the installation relies on a local connection to the ground (the rod), this is a TT system (Terra-Terra).
Step 2: Identify the 'Means of Earthing'. The copper-clad rod in the ground is the Earth Electrode.
Step 3: Identify the main connection cable. The 16mm² green and yellow cable connecting that rod to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) is the Earthing Conductor.
Step 4: Identify the circuit-level protection. The 1.5mm² green and yellow wire inside the 'twin and earth' cable feeding the workshop lights is the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC).

Step 5: Determine the required protective device. Because this is a TT system with high soil resistance, a standard circuit breaker might not trip fast enough. Therefore, a Residual Current Device (RCD) is required as part of the ADS strategy to ensure rapid disconnection.
Final Result: The installation is a TT system. The ADS components identified are the Earth Electrode (rod), the Earthing Conductor (main link), the CPC (circuit wire), and an RCD (protective device).

Takeaway: In a TT system, the consumer is responsible for providing the earth connection via an electrode, and an RCD is almost always essential to make the ADS system work correctly due to high earth resistance.
parsed.hint: Always look at the 'source' of the earth first. If it's a rod in the dirt and not a wire from the supplier, it's a TT system.

guidedPractice.title: Guided Practice
guidedPractice.objective: Identify a TT earthing system and its primary connection component based on a rural installation scenario.
retrievalTextLines: Recall the TT (Terra-Terra) system: the supplier provides no earth terminal.
The consumer must provide their own connection to the mass of earth via an electrode.
The 'Earthing Conductor' is the specific cable that links the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to that electrode.
guidedPractice.questionText: You are working on a remote farm building. The electricity supplier has provided a live and neutral connection, but no earth terminal. You install a copper-clad rod into the ground and run a 16mm² green and yellow cable from the rod to the building's main earthing terminal. Identify the earthing system type and the technical name of the 16mm² cable you just installed.
guidedPractice.hint: Think about the 'Terra-Terra' arrangement where both the source and the installation have their own separate connections to the ground.

practice.title: Practice
practice.objective: Identify a TN-C-S earthing system and the specific conductor used for circuit-level protection.
retrievalTextLines: TN-C-S systems use a combined neutral and earth (CNE) conductor in the supply cable.
The earth and neutral are separated at the consumer's intake (the cutout).
Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs) are the wires that run to individual points like sockets or lights.
practice.questionText: A suburban house is fed by a supply where the neutral and earth are combined in the street cable but split into two separate terminals at the property's cutout. Inside a kitchen circuit, a 1.5mm² green and yellow wire connects the metal cooker switch box to the consumer unit. Identify the earthing system type and the technical name for that 1.5mm² wire.
practice.hint: Focus on the 'Combined' then 'Separate' nature of the supply, and remember the specific term for the earth wire found inside a final circuit cable.

      "objective": "Identify the core components that make up an ADS system.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) is not a single device, but a coordinated safety strategy designed to prevent electric shock.         "The core parts of this system include the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC), which runs to individual points like sockets, and the Earthing Conductor, which connects the whole building to the earth source. Finally, it involves bonding conductors (main and supplementary) and, in some systems, an earth electrode (a rod in the ground).       ],
      "hint": "Think of ADS as a team of parts, including wires like the CPC and devices like fuses, rather than just one single component."

      "objective": "Distinguish between protective devices and bonding conductors within the ADS framework.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "In an ADS system, different components have very specific roles. Protective devices, such as fuses and Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCBs), are the 'switches' that automatically turn off the electricity when they detect a fault current that is too high. Their job is to break the circuit to stop the danger.",
        "Bonding conductors have a different purpose. Main protective bonding conductors connect incoming metal services, such as gas or water pipes, to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET). Supplementary bonding is used in high-risk areas like bathrooms to connect touchable metal parts together. Unlike a CPC, bonding conductors are not designed to carry fault current back from a broken appliance. Instead, they keep all metalwork at the same voltage (equipotential). This prevents a person from getting a shock if they touch two different metal surfaces at the same time during a fault."
      ],
      "hint": "Protective devices like MCBs cut the power, while bonding conductors keep metal pipes at the same voltage to prevent shocks between them."
    },

      "title": "TT Earthing Systems",
      "objective": "Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Earthing systems are identified by letter codes. In a TT system, the first 'T' stands for 'Terra' (the Latin word for Earth), indicating the source transformer is connected to earth. The second 'T' means the consumer's installation is also connected to earth via its own local connection.",
        "In a TT arrangement, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal to the property. Because of this, the consumer must provide their own 'means of earthing' by installing an earth electrode. This is typically a copper-clad steel rod hammered deep into the soil. Because the fault current has to travel through the actual mass of the earth (soil and rocks) to get back to the transformer, the resistance is much higher than in metallic systems. Consequently, TT systems almost always require a Residual Current Device (RCD) to ensure the power shuts off fast enough to be safe."
      ],

steps.title: ADS Component Overview
steps.objective: Identify the core components that make up an ADS system.
keyIdeas: The list of ADS components: CPC, main protective bonding, supplementary bonding, earthing conductor, protective devices, and earth electrode.
ADS is the primary method of protection against electric shock; knowing the parts is the first step to ensuring they are present.

Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) is not a single device, but a coordinated safety strategy designed to prevent electric shock. It works by ensuring that if a fault occurs, the power is cut off almost instantly. For ADS to function, several components must work together as a system.

The core parts of this system include the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC), which runs to individual points like sockets, and the Earthing Conductor, which connects the whole building to the earth source. It also includes protective devices like fuses or circuit breakers that physically trip the power. Finally, it involves bonding conductors (main and supplementary) and, in some systems, an earth electrode (a rod in the ground). If any one of these parts is missing or broken, the 'safety shield' of ADS may fail to protect the user during a fault.

steps.title: Bonding and Protective Devices
steps.objective: Distinguish between protective devices and bonding conductors within the ADS framework.
keyIdeas: The specific roles of protective devices (fuses/MCBs) versus bonding conductors (main and supplementary).
Confusing bonding with circuit protection leads to dangerous installation errors.

In an ADS system, different components have very specific roles. Protective devices, such as fuses and Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCBs), are the 'switches' that automatically turn off the electricity when they detect a fault current that is too high. Their job is to break the circuit to stop the danger.

Bonding conductors have a different purpose. Main protective bonding conductors connect incoming metal services, such as gas or water pipes, to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET). Supplementary bonding is used in high-risk areas like bathrooms to connect touchable metal parts together. Unlike a CPC, bonding conductors are not designed to carry fault current back from a broken appliance. Instead, they keep all metalwork at the same voltage (equipotential). This prevents a person from getting a shock if they touch two different metal surfaces at the same time during a fault.

steps.title: TT Earthing Systems
steps.objective: Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.
keyIdeas: TT systems rely on the mass of earth for the fault path, requiring a consumer-owned earth electrode.
TT systems are common in rural areas; failing to identify one means failing to install the necessary electrode.

Earthing systems are identified by letter codes. In a TT system, the first 'T' stands for 'Terra' (the Latin word for Earth), indicating the source transformer is connected to earth. The second 'T' means the consumer's installation is also connected to earth via its own local connection.

In a TT arrangement, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal to the property. Because of this, the consumer must provide their own 'means of earthing' by installing an earth electrode. This is typically a copper-clad steel rod hammered deep into the soil. Because the fault current has to travel through the actual mass of the earth (soil and rocks) to get back to the transformer, the resistance is much higher than in metallic systems. Consequently, TT systems almost always require a Residual Current Device (RCD) to ensure the power shuts off fast enough to be safe.

steps.title: TN-S and TN-C-S Systems
steps.objective: Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using the code letters and supply path.
keyIdeas: TN-S (Separate neutral and earth throughout) vs TN-C-S (Combined neutral and earth in the supply, separated at the intake).
The supply type dictates how the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) is connected to the supplier's equipment.

TN systems use the supplier's cable to provide a low-resistance metallic path for fault current. In a TN-S system, the 'S' stands for 'Separate'. This means the Neutral and Earth conductors are two completely distinct wires running all the way from the transformer to the consumer's intake. The earth is often provided by the metal sheath of the supplier's cable.

In a TN-C-S system, the 'C' stands for 'Combined' and the 'S' for 'Separate'. In this setup, the supplier uses one single wire (a PEN conductor) to act as both Neutral and Earth in the street. However, when this cable reaches the consumer's property, the combined wire is split into a separate Neutral and Earth at the cutout. This system is very common in the UK and is often referred to as PME (Protective Multiple Earthing) because the supplier earths the combined conductor at multiple points along the network.

steps.title: CPC and Earthing Conductor
steps.objective: Explain the difference between a CPC and an Earthing Conductor in ADS.
keyIdeas: The Earthing Conductor connects the MET to the means of earthing; the CPC connects the MET to the exposed parts of a circuit.
Using the wrong term during testing or installation leads to non-compliance and confusion in documentation.

It is vital to use the correct names for conductors to avoid confusion during testing and installation. The Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) is the specific wire that runs within an individual circuit. For example, the small green and yellow wire inside a 'twin and earth' cable that connects a metal light fitting to the consumer unit is a CPC.

The Earthing Conductor is different; it is the main, thick green and yellow wire that connects the entire installation's Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to the actual means of earthing (like the supplier's earth terminal or an earth rod). To remember the difference, think of the CPC as a 'branch' that protects one specific circuit, while the Earthing Conductor is the 'trunk' that connects the whole system's earth terminal to the ground source.

steps.title: Worked Example
steps.objective: Identify the earthing system type and the specific ADS components required for a rural installation.
keyIdeas: Show one worked example.

Scenario: You are inspecting a small detached workshop in a rural area. You notice that the electricity supplier's incoming cable (the cutout) does not have an earth terminal provided. Outside, you find a copper-clad steel rod driven into the ground with a 16mm² green and yellow cable connected to it, which runs back to a terminal block inside the consumer unit.

Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Because the supplier has not provided an earth terminal and the installation relies on a local connection to the ground (the rod), this is a TT system (Terra-Terra).
Step 2: Identify the 'Means of Earthing'. The copper-clad rod in the ground is the Earth Electrode.
Step 3: Identify the main connection cable. The 16mm² green and yellow cable connecting that rod to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) is the Earthing Conductor.
Step 4: Identify the circuit-level protection. The 1.5mm² green and yellow wire inside the 'twin and earth' cable feeding the workshop lights is the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC).

steps.title: Integrative Close: Why TT Uses an RCD
steps.objective: Explain why TT systems usually need an RCD.
keyIdeas: TT systems rely on soil for the return path, which has much higher resistance than a metal return path.
High earth path resistance limits fault current, so a fuse or MCB may not trip quickly enough.
An RCD is used because it trips on imbalance rather than needing a large fault current.
anchorFacts: High soil resistance means low fault current in a TT system.
An RCD can disconnect even when the fault current is too small for a fuse or MCB to operate quickly.

spacedReview.title: Spaced Review: Circuit Protection Fundamentals
spacedReview.objective: Recall the primary function of protective devices and the basic path of a fault current from prerequisite 203-3C.
retrievalTextLines: A protective device, such as an MCB or fuse, is designed to disconnect a circuit when current exceeds its rated limit.
In a 'fault to earth' scenario, the metalwork of an appliance becomes live.
For the protective device to trip, there must be a complete, low-resistance path for that fault current to flow back to the transformer.
If this path is broken or has high resistance, the protective device will not 'see' the fault, leaving the metalwork dangerously energized.
spacedReview.questionText: What is the primary purpose of a circuit protective device (like a fuse or MCB) when a line conductor touches a metal enclosure?
answerGuidance: To achieve automatic disconnection of the supply (ADS).
To cut off the power quickly enough to prevent a fatal electric shock.
To detect the high fault current and open the circuit.
